metadata.title: The 12-Steps and 12 Traditions Glossary
metadata.description: Comprehensive glossary of terms from AA's 12 Steps and 12 Traditions, including archaic words, AA-specific concepts, principles, and proper nouns. All entries organized alphabetically.

A.A..name: A.A.
A.A..definition: Abbreviation for Alcoholics Anonymous; the global fellowship of people recovering from alcoholism.

abashed.definition: Embarrassed or ashamed, feeling put down or disconcerted. In the 12x12, used to describe the uncomfortable recognition of one's own defects buried under layers of self-justification.

abetted.definition: Assisted or encouraged someone in wrongdoing or self-deception. In the 12x12, describes how the world's failure to understand alcoholism enables the alcoholic's denial.

abhor.definition: To regard with intense disgust or hatred; to detest utterly. Used in the 12x12 to convey the depth of revulsion toward self-destruction, as in the statement that God and nature abhor suicide.

absolve.definition: To formally declare someone free from guilt, responsibility, or blame. In the 12x12, refers to the alcoholic's tendency to excuse themselves from accountability by casting blame outward.

abstractions.definition: Concepts or ideas that exist in the mind rather than as concrete, tangible realities. In the 12x12, used to contrast dry intellectual understanding of spiritual principles with lived, practical experience.

absurdly.definition: In a manner that is ridiculously unreasonable or illogical. Used in the 12x12 to highlight the irrational nature of certain alcoholic behaviors and thinking patterns.

abundance.definition: A very large quantity or plenty. A future of abundance is promised by progress.

acceptance.definition: The action of accepting; receiving something willingly. In AA, acceptance means three things: acknowledging your powerlessness over alcohol, believing a Higher Power can help, and tolerating things you cannot change (like other people's behavior). Acceptance is the opposite of fighting reality.

accomplishment.definition: The successful completion of a task. Accomplishing recovery through united effort.

accountability.definition: Being answerable for one's behavior; taking responsibility. Part of making amends.

achievement.definition: Successful accomplishment; reaching a goal. Achievement of sobriety is emphasized in AA as coming through the Steps, not personal willpower.

acid test.definition: A crucial or decisive test (originally from chemistry - acid was used to test if metal was genuine gold). In AA, the acid test of Step Ten is whether you can stay sober and live well under all conditions.

acme.definition: The highest point of achievement or excellence; the peak. In the 12x12, used to describe the summit of spiritual or character development that AA members strive toward.

acquaintance.definition: Knowledge or familiarity with something. Most people lack even a nodding acquaintance with humility.

activator.definition: Something or someone that initiates or energizes a process or action. In the 12x12, used to describe forces or steps that set spiritual growth and recovery in motion.

actors.definition: People who perform roles or act. Alcoholics sometimes minimize Step Five by admitting to being 'bad actors.'

admission.definition: The act of acknowledging or confessing something; declaring acceptance. Admissions of powerlessness and defeat are essential to beginning recovery.

advancement.definition: Moving forward; promotion or improvement. In spiritual and personal matters.

advantage.definition: Favorable circumstance or benefit; superiority. Taking advantage of recovery.

adventuring.definition: Engaging in bold, risky, or exciting undertakings. In the 12x12, used to describe the spirit of openness and willingness required when exploring new spiritual ground in recovery.

adversity.definition: Difficult or unfortunate circumstances. In adversity, faith sustains one through reliance on Higher Power.

advertises.definition: Makes something widely known; promotes or publicizes. In the 12x12, used to describe how certain behaviors or attitudes broadcast one's character defects to others.

affirmative.definition: Confirming; asserting 'yes'; positive action. Step Three calls for affirmative action to turn over will to God.

ageless.definition: Not appearing to age; timeless or eternal. Used in the 12x12 to describe spiritual truths or principles that remain relevant and vital across generations.

aggravate.definition: To make worse; to worsen. Character defects cause or aggravate pressing problems.

aggrieved.definition: Feeling resentment or hurt due to perceived unfair treatment. In the 12x12, describes the alcoholic's tendency to nurse a sense of injury and righteousness that fuels resentment.

agnostics.definition: People who neither believe nor disbelieve in God, holding that ultimate truth is unknowable. In the 12x12, specifically addressed as a group for whom AA's spiritual program can still be effective without requiring orthodox belief.

agonized.definition: Suffered or caused great mental or physical pain; tormented. Used in the 12x12 to describe the intense suffering that drives an alcoholic to desperately seek recovery.

aid.definition: Help or assistance; providing help. Mutual aid among members.

aimlessly.definition: Without purpose, direction, or goal. In the 12x12, describes the rudderless existence of the alcoholic who has not yet found a framework of principles to live by.

airtight.definition: Having no flaws or weaknesses; completely sealed against any objection or escape. Used in the 12x12 to describe rationalizations the alcoholic constructs to justify behavior.

alcoholic.definition: A person suffering from alcoholism; someone unable to control their drinking and for whom alcohol causes problems. The term also refers collectively to AA members - 'alcoholics' in recovery.

Alcoholics Anonymous.name: Alcoholics Anonymous
Alcoholics Anonymous.definition: The full name of AA; a worldwide mutual aid fellowship for recovering alcoholics, founded in 1935.

alcoholism.definition: A chronic disease characterized by an inability to control drinking, physical dependence on alcohol, and continuing to drink despite harmful consequences. The 12x12 describes it as an 'age-old, baffling malady' — meaning it is ancient, deeply puzzling, and resistant to ordinary remedies of willpower or medicine alone.

alibis.definition: Excuses or justifications offered to avoid blame; claims of innocence. In AA, refers to the elaborate explanations alcoholics construct to avoid accountability for their drinking and behavior.

allayed.definition: Reduced or eliminated fear, suspicion, or worry; relieved or soothed. Used in the 12x12 to describe how honest sharing and fellowship can diminish anxiety and dread.

allergy.definition: In AA, a physical abnormality in how the alcoholic's body reacts to alcohol (borrowed from medical terminology for immune overreaction). Like a shellfish allergy - where the body reacts badly to something harmless to others - an alcoholic's body can never safely tolerate alcohol, even in small amounts. Combined with mental obsession, it makes recovery impossible without outside help.

alternated.definition: Went back and forth between two states or actions in turns. In the 12x12, used to describe the oscillation between extremes, such as grandiosity and self-contempt, common in alcoholic thinking.

ambition.definition: Strong desire to succeed; a goal. Personal ambitions are set aside for recovery work.

amends.definition: Making right past wrongs through apology and changed behavior. Not just saying sorry, but demonstrating change and attempting to repair harm - central to Steps 8-9.

anger.definition: A strong feeling of displeasure or hostility. One of the negative emotions that create emotional hangovers.

angle.definition: A hidden reason or point of view. The common question, 'There must be an angle somewhere.'

animates.definition: Gives life, energy, or spirit to something; motivates or inspires. Used in the 12x12 to describe how spiritual principles or God's will infuse purpose and vitality into recovery.

anonymity.definition: The state of not being identified by name; the principle that conceals member identities at public level. 'Anonymity is the spiritual foundation of all our traditions.'

anvils.definition: Heavy iron blocks on which metal is hammered and shaped; symbols of hard, transformative work. In the 12x12, used metaphorically for the difficulties that shape and forge character in recovery.

apartness.definition: The state of being separate or isolated from others; a sense of not belonging. In the 12x12, describes the alcoholic's painful feeling of difference and disconnection from humanity.

appeals.definition: Requests for help or sympathy. Too many personal appeals would overwhelm AA if known.

appearance.definition: The act of coming into view; the way something looks. When the Big Book appeared in 1939.

appraise.definition: To assess or evaluate the worth, quality, or nature of something. In the 12x12, used in the context of taking a searching moral inventory and honestly evaluating one's character.

appraising.definition: Evaluating or judging the quality or nature of something with careful assessment. In the 12x12, used in the context of honest, searching self-examination required in Step Four.

approval.definition: Official or informal agreement; acceptance. Corporations' approval enhances AA's credibility.

arouse.definition: To awaken, stir up, or evoke a feeling or response. In the 12x12, used to describe how certain situations trigger character defects like anger, lust, or fear.

arouses.definition: Stirs up, awakens, or evokes a feeling or response in others. In the 12x12, used to describe how behavior or character defects provoke reactions in relationships.

arrant.definition: Complete and utter; used to emphasize the extreme degree of a negative quality. Used in the 12x12 to describe thoroughgoing self-deception or dishonesty.

arteries.definition: Vessels carrying blood from the heart. Used metaphorically for AA's vital channels.

articles.definition: Written pieces in publications. Articles in church journals prompt inquiry.

as we understood Him.definition: Phrase allowing each person to define their conception of a Higher Power. Intentionally left vague to include atheists (group as HP), agnostics (unknowable HP), religious (traditional God), and all others.

assailed.definition: Attacked vigorously with arguments, questions, or negative feelings; bombarded. In the 12x12, describes how the newcomer or struggling member may be beset by doubt, fear, or criticism.

assets.definition: Valuable resources or advantageous qualities. Instincts are great natural assets when properly directed.

assistance.definition: Help or support offered. Assistance in the recovery process.

astonish.definition: To surprise or impress greatly; to fill with wonder. Used in the 12x12 to describe the remarkable, unexpected nature of spiritual awakening and recovery.

astounded.definition: Greatly surprised or shocked; overwhelmed with amazement. In the 12x12, used to convey the startling nature of spiritual experiences and the depth of transformation possible in recovery.

asunder.definition: Into separate pieces; apart or divided. In the 12x12, used to describe the tearing apart of relationships or the dissolution of harmful attitudes through rigorous honesty.

asylums.definition: Institutions for the care of people with mental illness; historical term for psychiatric hospitals. In the 12x12, referenced as one of the endpoints awaiting the untreated alcoholic.

atheistic.definition: Relating to or characterized by the rejection of belief in God or gods. In the 12x12, specifically addressed because many alcoholics who identify as atheists are shown that AA's program can work without conventional religious belief.

attainment.definition: The act of achieving or reaching a goal. The attainment of greater humility is the goal of Step Seven.

attention.definition: Notice or focus of mind; awareness. Paying attention in meetings.

attraction.definition: The power to draw or appeal to someone. AA grows through attraction, not promotion.

attraction rather than promotion.definition: Growing through appeal and example rather than active marketing. AA's public relations principle.

authenticity.definition: Quality of being authentic and genuine. Genuine recovery versus false recovery.

authority.definition: The power to make decisions or enforce obedience. In AA, authority rests only with a loving God.

autonomous.definition: Self-governing; independent; having autonomy. Each AA group should be autonomous.

autonomy.definition: The quality of self-governance; independence; self-direction. A fundamental principle in Tradition Four.

availed.definition: Made use of; took advantage of an opportunity or resource. In the 12x12, used to describe the willingness to draw on AA's program, fellowship, and spiritual principles.

backing.definition: Support or endorsement; being behind something. Having backing of fellowship.

backslider.definition: A person who returns to wrongdoing or sin after a period of improvement; one who relapses. In AA, refers to a member who returns to drinking after a period of sobriety.

backtracks.definition: Returns to a previous position or abandons a principle previously held. In the 12x12, used to describe retreating from spiritual progress or honest commitments.

baffling.definition: Impossible to understand or explain; utterly perplexing. The 12x12 uses this word to describe the nature of alcoholism — that it defies rational explanation and cannot be overcome by willpower or logic alone, which is why a spiritual solution is required.

baited.definition: Lured, provoked, or teased in order to produce a desired reaction; set a trap. In the 12x12, used to describe how pride or anger can be triggered or exploited.

balance.definition: State of being in equilibrium. Balance in all areas of life.

baleful.definition: Threatening harm or evil; menacing in a harmful way. Used in the 12x12 to describe dangerous emotional states or destructive forces that threaten recovery.

balk.definition: To refuse or hesitate to accept something; to stop short and refuse to proceed. In the 12x12, describes the alcoholic's resistance to surrendering ego or accepting guidance.

balked.definition: Refused to proceed or accept something; stopped short. In the 12x12, describes the moment when pride or fear causes a member to recoil from a necessary step.

bankruptcy.definition: Financial or moral insolvency; complete failure. In AA, refers to the complete spiritual and personal bankruptcy that precedes recovery.

barest.definition: Most minimal or spare; the very least amount of something. Used in the 12x12 to describe the minimum requirement of willingness or humility needed to begin recovery.

barrooms.definition: Rooms in a tavern or bar where alcohol is served. In the 12x12, referenced as environments that were central to the alcoholic's former life and are now to be navigated with caution.

bartenders.definition: People who serve drinks at a bar. In the 12x12, referenced as part of the drinking world's social fabric familiar to the alcoholic before recovery.

basis.definition: The main reason or underlying principle. Basis of AA recovery.

bask.definition: To lie or relax in a pleasant warmth or pleasant feeling; to revel in attention. In the 12x12, used to describe resting comfortably in spiritual well-being or enjoying deserved approval.

battering.definition: Striking repeatedly and violently; subjecting to repeated harsh treatment. In the 12x12, used to describe the relentless assault of character defects, compulsions, or emotional difficulties.

beached.definition: Stranded on a shore; brought to a standstill and unable to move forward. In the 12x12, used metaphorically to describe being stuck or helpless without spiritual resources.

beachhead.definition: A secure initial position that has been gained and from which further advances can be made. In the 12x12, used to describe the early foothold of sobriety or faith from which spiritual growth can proceed.

beatitudes.definition: The blessings declared by Jesus in the Sermon on the Mount, beginning with 'Blessed are...'. In the 12x12, referenced as expressions of spiritual virtues—humility, meekness, mercy—that parallel AA's spiritual ideals.

bedeviled.definition: Tormented, harassed, or greatly troubled; plagued by difficulties. In the 12x12, describes how character defects and emotional problems persistently torment the alcoholic.

bedrock.definition: Solid rock underlying soil; a firm foundation. The foundation of recovery built on admitting powerlessness.

beehive.definition: A structure housing a colony of bees; metaphorically, a busy, productive place. In the 12x12, used to describe a scene of bustling communal activity within AA groups.

begats.definition: Engenders or gives rise to; the biblical term for fathering offspring. In the 12x12, used to describe how one spiritual quality or defect gives birth to another.

beginning.definition: First part or start; commencement. Beginning of recovery journey.

beginning of the end.definition: A turning point where an old way of life is ending and something new is beginning. Used to show that the sponsor's laugh signals the start of the end of the newcomer's old life and the start of their recovery.

behavior.definition: The way one acts or conducts oneself. Drinking behavior must be examined and changed.

belaboring.definition: Arguing or explaining a point at excessive, unnecessary length. In the 12x12, refers to the danger of overworking or dwelling excessively on a grievance or idea.

belligerent.definition: Aggressive, hostile, or ready to fight. Describes the combative stance some take against Step Two.

benders.definition: Extended periods of heavy, uncontrolled drinking. In AA, a familiar term for the drinking sprees that define out-of-control alcoholism.

benefactors.definition: People who give financial or other aid; supporters or patrons. In the 12x12, used to describe those who have contributed to AA's growth or to an individual's recovery journey.

benefit.definition: An advantage or improvement; something helpful. Benefits of following Steps.

bereavement.definition: The state of sorrow over the death of a loved one; deep grief from loss. In the 12x12, referenced as one of the painful life events that can shake sobriety if not met with spiritual resources.

besetting.definition: Constantly troubling or harassing; surrounding on all sides. In the 12x12, used to describe persistent character defects or temptations that continually afflict the alcoholic.

bestowal.definition: The act of giving or conferring something as a gift or honor. In the 12x12, used to describe the giving of spiritual gifts—such as sobriety, serenity, and grace—by a Higher Power.

Big Book.name: Big Book
Big Book.definition: The primary AA text, officially titled 'Alcoholics Anonymous,' published in 1939. Contains the Twelve Steps and recovery stories.

binges.definition: Periods of excessive indulgence, especially in drinking. In AA, refers to the episodes of uncontrolled alcoholic drinking that are hallmarks of the disease.

bleeds.definition: To drain or deplete something steadily, as if drawing blood. Used figuratively: alcohol 'bleeds' the alcoholic of self-sufficiency — gradually stripping away every capacity for self-direction, willpower, and independence, leaving nothing behind.

blessings.definition: Benefits or gifts; divine favor. Material blessings from technology and industry.

blinder.definition: More blind; less able to see clearly or perceive truth. In the 12x12, used comparatively to describe increasing self-deception or inability to see one's own defects.

bludgeoned.definition: Beaten with a heavy weapon or overwhelming force; subjected to relentless pressure. In the 12x12, describes how a person may be driven to change by the accumulated weight of suffering.

bluffing.definition: Pretending to have strength, knowledge, or ability one does not possess; deceiving. In the 12x12, describes the alcoholic's self-deceptive performance of confidence or control.

blunders.definition: Serious mistakes or errors due to carelessness or lack of judgment. In the 12x12, refers to the moral and practical errors that stem from character defects and alcoholic thinking.

board.definition: A group governing an organization. AA has no board of directors with power to expel members.

bogeymen.definition: Scary phantoms or imaginary fears that seem very real and threatening but aren't (from folklore about creatures used to frighten children). In Step Four, pride and fear feel like towering barriers, but AA says they're bogeymen - once you actually start the inventory, they lose all their power.

bogged.definition: Stuck in a bog; unable to make progress; mired in difficulty. In the 12x12, describes becoming hopelessly stuck in self-pity, resentment, or confusion.

bombard.definition: To attack relentlessly with something overwhelming; to subject to a barrage. In the 12x12, describes how doubts, fears, or temptations can assault the recovering alcoholic.

booby.definition: Foolish or stupid; a person regarded as foolish. In the 12x12, used colloquially to describe a trap or pitfall, as in 'booby trap,' referring to hidden dangers in recovery.

bookshelf.definition: A shelf for holding books. In the 12x12, used literally to reference the spiritual and recovery literature available to AA members.

boomeranged.definition: Returned to cause harm to the originator; backfired. In the 12x12, used to describe how dishonest or selfish actions return to hurt the person who initiated them.

braggart.definition: A person who boasts loudly and excessively about their achievements. In the 12x12, used to describe the egotism and self-inflation that can mask deeper insecurity in the alcoholic.

brainpower.definition: Intellectual capacity or mental ability. In the 12x12, used to describe the alcoholic's misplaced reliance on intelligence rather than spiritual resources.

brash.definition: Self-assertive in a rude, aggressive way; hasty and unthinking. In the 12x12, describes the impulsive, overconfident approach to life that characterizes alcoholic behavior.

brats.definition: Spoiled, badly behaved children; used contemptuously. In the 12x12, used to describe the childish, demanding behavior that characterizes unchecked self-will.

breadwinner.definition: The member of a household who earns the primary income. In the 12x12, referenced in the context of financial and family responsibility that alcoholism endangers.

breakable.definition: Capable of being broken; fragile. In the 12x12, used to describe the fragility of sobriety and the importance of protecting the recovery relationship with humility.

breakups.definition: Endings of relationships; separations. In the 12x12, referenced as painful life events that can threaten sobriety if not navigated with spiritual support.

broods.definition: Thinks deeply and at length about something that makes one unhappy; dwells moodily. In the 12x12, describes the destructive pattern of obsessive, negative rumination common to alcoholics.

brotherhood.definition: Feeling of fellowship and kinship; bond between members. AA's community of recovering alcoholics.

browbeat.definition: To intimidate or bully with persistent harsh criticism; to dominate overbearingly. In the 12x12, describes the domineering behavior that stems from self-will run riot.

buffeted.definition: Struck repeatedly; battered or knocked about by opposing forces. In the 12x12, describes how the alcoholic is tossed about by emotional storms and circumstances.

businesswomen.definition: Women engaged in commerce or trade. In the 12x12, referenced to acknowledge AA's reach across gender and professional lines.

busybody.definition: A person who meddles in the affairs of others; an intrusive, nosy person. In the 12x12, describes the controlling tendency that stems from self-will and the belief that one knows best.

buttonholing.definition: Stopping and detaining someone in conversation against their will; cornering someone. In the 12x12, used to describe the overeager or overbearing approach to Twelfth Step work.

bygones.definition: Past events, especially those that should be forgotten. In the 12x12, used in the context of letting go of old grievances and resentments in order to live freely in the present.

bypass.definition: To go around; to avoid. Many try to bypass Step Five by making only partial admissions.

calamities.definition: Events causing great harm, distress, or misery; disasters. In the 12x12, used to describe the catastrophic consequences that alcoholism brings to individuals, families, and communities.

cancer.definition: A disease; used metaphorically for the human condition without spiritual principle. Metaphor for alcoholism.

candor.definition: The quality of being open and honest in expression; frank, straightforward speech. In the 12x12, identified as essential to the Twelve Step process, especially in Step Five.

capsize.definition: To overturn or cause to overturn in water; to be overwhelmed and destroyed. In the 12x12, used metaphorically to describe how emotional turmoil can swamp and destroy recovery.

care.definition: Serious attention or consideration; looking after. Care for fellowmembers.

careers.definition: Occupations or professions; courses of life. Step Five involves examining career mistakes and harms.

caretakers.definition: People who take care of others or manage something. In the 12x12, used to describe either AA members who serve the group or the enabling roles that family members can fall into.

carry out.definition: To execute or perform; to put into effect. Praying for power to carry out God's will.

carry the message.definition: To bring the AA message of recovery to alcoholics. The primary function of AA service.

caution.definition: Careful attention to avoid danger; wariness. Exercising caution when making amends.

caves.definition: Large underground chambers. Reference to where alcoholics would be 'back again in their caves' without AA.

ceaselessly.definition: Without stopping; continuously. In the 12x12, describes the unrelenting nature of the spiritual search, the work of recovery, or the persistence of certain character defects.

centeredness.definition: The quality of being focused, balanced, and spiritually grounded. In the 12x12, used to describe the stability and peace that comes from living by spiritual principles.

chalking.definition: Marking or scoring; recording. In the 12x12, used in the phrase 'chalking it up' to mean attributing something to a particular cause or writing it off to experience.

challengingly.definition: In a manner that provokes, dares, or tests. In the 12x12, describes the confrontational or testing tone that can arise in spiritual exploration or interpersonal conflict.

channeled.definition: Directed or guided along a particular path. In the 12x12, describes how emotional energy or self-will can be redirected from selfish ends toward service and spiritual growth.

character.definition: The basic makeup of who you are - your habits, values, and how you treat others. The core of your personality.

character assets.definition: Positive qualities one possesses - honesty, courage, compassion, integrity, wisdom. Step work helps identify and use these strengths rather than focusing only on flaws.

character growth.definition: Development of positive character traits and virtues. Result of working the Steps.

characteristically.definition: In a way that is typical or distinctive of a person or thing. Used in the 12x12 to describe behavior patterns that are recognizably and consistently alcoholic.

characterizes.definition: Describes or distinguishes the essential qualities of something. In the 12x12, used to identify the defining features of alcoholic thinking or spiritual defects.

charitably.definition: In a manner showing generosity, goodwill, and kindness toward others. In the 12x12, describes the spirit of generosity and tolerance AA members are encouraged to bring to relationships.

charity.definition: Generous giving to help those in need; goodwill toward others. Service to suffering alcoholics.

cheerfully.definition: In a noticeably happy and optimistic manner. In the 12x12, describes the attitude of willing acceptance and joyful service that characterizes healthy recovery.

cherished.definition: Held with deep affection and care; treasured above other things. 'The unity of A.A. is the most cherished quality our Society has' — unity is not merely useful, it is loved and protected as something precious, without which AA could not survive.

child.definition: A young person; offspring. Anonymity was 'the child of our early fears.'

childishly.definition: In a manner typical of a child; immature, petulant, or irrationally demanding. In the 12x12, used to describe the alcoholic's emotional immaturity and self-centered reactions.

chink.definition: A small gap or crack; a weakness in an otherwise solid defense. In the 12x12, used to describe the vulnerable point through which character defects or denial can penetrate.

chokedamp.definition: A mixture of gases, mainly carbon dioxide, found in mines after an explosion or fire; also called blackdamp. In the 12x12, used as a metaphor for the spiritual atmosphere of guilt and despair that can suffocate recovery.

churchman.definition: A male member of a church, especially one who is active and devout. In the 12x12, referenced as a counterpoint to the alcoholic to illustrate how spiritual principles apply across backgrounds.

cinch.definition: Informal: something absolutely certain; a sure thing; guaranteed. 'It's a cinch we never shall publicly' — meaning it is completely certain that AA will not argue publicly about outside issues, so long as members also avoid those arguments privately.

clamor.definition: A loud, sustained noise or vociferous protest; a demand made urgently. In the 12x12, describes the loud insistence of self-will or the clamoring of unmet emotional needs.

clamored.definition: Made a loud, persistent noise or demand; protested vigorously. In the 12x12, describes the urgent, persistent demands of self-will and ego.

clapboard.definition: A narrow board used in overlapping rows to cover the exterior walls of buildings. In the 12x12, referenced as a detail of modest, everyday American settings familiar to AA members.

clarity.definition: Quality of being clear; lucidity. Clarity through recovery.

classes.definition: Groups or categories of people. Step Nine amends fall into several classes based on circumstances.

clergyman.definition: A man who is ordained as a religious minister or priest. Historical reference to spiritual authority.

clergymen.definition: Ordained ministers, priests, rabbis, or other religious leaders. Many newcomers initially regard prayer and meditation as a mysterious practice belonging only to clergymen — trained religious professionals — rather than something available to any person in recovery.

clinked.definition: Made or caused to make a light, sharp ringing sound. In the 12x12, referenced in the context of glasses of alcohol clinking, evoking drinking culture.

clubhouses.definition: Buildings used by a club or organization for meetings and social events. In the 12x12, used to describe AA meeting places that serve as hubs of fellowship and community.

clubs.definition: Organizations or gathering places. AA clubs serve as meeting places.

clung.definition: Held on tightly; refused to let go. In the 12x12, describes the stubborn grip on self-will, old ideas, or harmful patterns that blocks spiritual progress.

codified.definition: Arranged laws or principles into a systematic code; formally systematized. In the 12x12, used to describe the way AA's Twelve Traditions were formalized into guiding principles for group governance.

coldness.definition: Lack of warmth, affection, or friendliness; emotional distance. In the 12x12, describes the emotional withdrawal and distance that can harm relationships and block spiritual connection.

comers.definition: People or things that are new and show promise. In the 12x12, used to describe newcomers to AA or to describe those who are advancing in the program.

comings.definition: Arrivals or returns; the act of coming. In the 12x12, used in the phrase 'comings and goings' to describe the transient nature of members' engagement with the program.

commence.definition: To begin; to start something formally and deliberately. A more formal word than 'begin,' suggesting an intentional, purposeful start. 'We commence to put our A.A. way of living to practical use' — Step Ten marks the point where daily practice truly begins.

commencement.definition: Beginning or start; ceremony marking beginning. Commencement of recovery.

commences.definition: Begins; starts. Used in the 12x12 to mark the start of a new spiritual process or phase of recovery.

commitment.definition: Pledge or promise; dedication to a cause. Commitment to recovery and helping others.

committeemen.definition: Members of a committee responsible for making decisions or governing a body. In the 12x12, referenced in the context of AA's group conscience and service structure.

committees.definition: Groups appointed or elected to perform specific functions. AA may establish committees for service work.

common welfare.definition: The well-being or good of the whole community. Should come first; personal recovery depends on it.

companionship.definition: Friendly association with others; the desire for company. A legitimate human desire when not misdirected.

compassion.definition: Sympathetic concern for others' suffering; empathy. The basis of service and helping others.

complications.definition: Difficulties that make a situation harder; complex problems layered on top of each other. 'No matter how grave your emotional complications' — referring to deep psychological or emotional troubles a person carries into AA. Tradition Three says none of these can disqualify someone from membership.

composed.definition: Made up of; consisting of. 'A society composed entirely of alcoholics' — a fellowship whose members are all alcoholics, which makes its financial self-discipline particularly remarkable and newsworthy.

comprehension.definition: Ability to understand; grasping meaning. Comprehension of principles.

compulsions.definition: Irresistible urges to behave in a particular way, especially against one's conscious wishes. In AA, refers to the driving force of addiction and the impulse toward self-defeating behaviors.

conceit.definition: Excessive pride in oneself and one's abilities; vanity. In the 12x12, identified as a key character defect that distorts perception and blocks honest self-appraisal.

conceited.definition: Excessively proud of oneself; vain. In the 12x12, used to describe the self-inflated attitude that masks insecurity in the alcoholic.

conceivable.definition: Capable of being imagined or thought of; possible within the range of human imagination. 'Every conceivable deviation has been tried' — meaning every variation, shortcut, and modification that could possibly be thought up has already been attempted in AA's history.

concentration.definition: Fixing attention; focusing on one thing. Concentration on Steps.

concern.definition: Worry or anxiety; care for something. Concern for others' recovery.

concerted.definition: Jointly planned and carried out; done together in coordination and cooperation. 'Upon whose concerted work would depend the answer for sufferers' — meaning the united, coordinated effort of the whole group, not any individual working alone.

conclusions.definition: Decisions or judgments reached after consideration; the end point of thinking something through. In Step Three's context, the conclusions — that one is powerless and needs a Higher Power — require not action but simple acceptance and willingness.

conferring.definition: Consulting or discussing with others before making a decision; seeking advice or agreement. The Long Form of Tradition Nine states that no group should take major actions affecting AA as a whole without first conferring — consulting — with the General Service Board.

confide.definition: To reveal a secret or private matter to someone, trusting them to keep it. In the 12x12, refers to the intimate sharing in Step Five, disclosing one's wrongs to another person.

confided.definition: Revealed a secret or personal information to someone in trust. In the 12x12, used to describe the courageous act of disclosing one's inventory in Step Five.

confidence.definition: Trust or belief in someone or something. AA's sacrifices give people confidence in the future.

confiding.definition: Sharing secrets or private matters in trust. In the 12x12, describes the trusting, vulnerable sharing essential to Step Five and to genuine fellowship.

conformity.definition: Compliance with rules, norms, or the expectations of others; going along with established standards. 'Membership must never depend on money or conformity' — AA requires neither financial contribution nor agreement with any particular belief system or behavior as a condition of belonging.

connives.definition: Plans or acts secretly with others to do something harmful; schemes. In the 12x12, describes the dishonest, manipulative behavior driven by self-will and character defects.

conscience.definition: Internal sense of right and wrong; moral awareness. Part of 'group conscience.'

conscious contact.definition: Aware and direct connection or relationship. Step Eleven seeks to improve conscious contact with God.

consequences.definition: Results or effects of actions; the outcomes of drinking and dishonest living.

consideration.definition: Thoughtful care for others; taking into account. When making amends.

consistency.definition: Quality of being consistent; regularity. Consistent practice of Steps.

consternation.definition: A feeling of anxiety, dismay, or great shock. In the 12x12, describes the alarmed reaction to one's own character defects discovered through honest self-examination.

constructively.definition: In a way that produces improvement or positive results. In the 12x12, describes the healthy channeling of energy and criticism toward building rather than tearing down.

contempt.definition: Feelings of disrespect or disdain. Ex-drinkers feared contempt from society.

contemptuous.definition: Feeling or showing contempt; scornful and disrespectful. In the 12x12, describes an attitude of disdain—either directed at others or received from others—that damages relationships and spiritual health.

contemptuously.definition: In a manner showing contempt or scorn; dismissively. In the 12x12, describes how alcoholics can dismiss spiritual ideas or other people with arrogant disdain.

contented.definition: Satisfied with one's situation; not desiring more or different. In the 12x12, describes the state of spiritual peace and acceptance that recovery makes possible.

contentment.definition: State of being satisfied and at peace. From living by spiritual principles.

contradiction.definition: An apparent conflict between two things that seem to oppose each other; a paradox. 'In seeming contradiction' — Tradition Nine says AA is organized as little as possible, yet still creates service boards. This appears to contradict itself but actually resolves into a workable tension.

controlled drinking.definition: The hope that an alcoholic can drink "normally" or in moderation. AA says this is impossible for true alcoholics - the allergy and obsession make control impossible no matter how much willpower is used.

controversial.definition: Causing strong disagreement or public debate; relating to a subject on which people hold opposing views. Tradition Ten keeps AA out of all controversial matters — politics, religion, reform — so that no dispute can divide the Fellowship or distract it from its primary purpose.

conviction.definition: Strong belief or firmly held opinion. Arrived at through reflection in Steps.

cooperation.definition: Working together for mutual benefit; teamwork. Essential in group function and recovery.

core.definition: Central or most important part. Core of the recovery program.

corporations.definition: Large business organizations. Great corporations have set their approval on AA.

cost.definition: Price paid; sacrifice required. Whatever personal cost, members must devote themselves to recovery.

courage.definition: The ability to face fear or difficulty; bravery in facing one's wrongs. Required for Steps Five and Nine.

covet.definition: To yearn to possess something belonging to another; to desire enviously. In the 12x12, used to describe the envy and greed that arise from the instinct for security and prestige run amok.

cowed.definition: Frightened or intimidated into submission. In the 12x12, describes how fear or domineering behavior can suppress honest expression and prevent spiritual growth.

crackerjack.definition: A person or thing of exceptional quality or skill; excellent. In the 12x12, used colloquially to describe someone highly capable or a plan that seems foolproof.

crackpot.definition: An eccentric person whose ideas are foolish or impractical. In the 12x12, used to describe how AA's spiritual ideas might initially appear to skeptics.

crackpots.definition: Eccentric or foolish people with impractical ideas. In the 12x12, used colloquially to describe people whose unconventional thinking is later recognized as wisdom.

creation.definition: The universe and all living things, understood as brought into existence by God or a Higher Power; also the act of divine creation itself. 'Creation gave us instincts for a purpose' — used in a theological sense, meaning the Higher Power designed human beings with natural drives for a reason.

Creator.definition: One who creates; in AA, referring to God. Growing in 'the image and likeness of his own Creator.'

crediting.definition: Attributing an achievement or quality to a source; giving recognition. In the 12x12, refers to acknowledging a Higher Power or the program as the source of recovery rather than taking personal credit.

creditor.definition: One to whom money is owed. In Step One, alcohol is described as a 'rapacious creditor' that bleeds those addicted of self-sufficiency.

cure.definition: A remedy for disease; restoration to health. AA found a cure for alcoholism.

custodians.definition: People who have responsibility for looking after or protecting something. In the 12x12, used to describe AA members' role as stewards and guardians of the program's principles.

dammed.definition: Blocked or held back by a barrier. In the 12x12, used metaphorically to describe emotions, spiritual growth, or potential that has been restrained and prevented from flowing freely.

darkening.definition: Becoming darker; growing more threatening or gloomy. In the 12x12, used to describe the progression of the alcoholic's situation as the disease deepens.

darkness.definition: Absence of light; obscurity. Reference to being cast into 'outer darkness' by authoritarian organizations.

dawns.definition: Begins to become apparent; grows clear. In the 12x12, describes the gradual emergence of spiritual understanding or the slow arrival of insight.

deacons.definition: Ordained officers of a church who assist the minister; lay officials who assist in church administration. In the 12x12, referenced as examples of people in religious service to contrast with or illustrate spiritual principles.

debits.definition: Entries recording money owed or expenses; losses or disadvantages on a ledger. In the 12x12, used in the moral inventory context as a metaphor for wrongs, defects, and debts that need to be acknowledged.

debris.definition: The scattered remains of something broken or destroyed; wreckage. Used figuratively in Steps Eight and Nine: the 'debris of the past' refers to the accumulated wreckage of broken relationships, unpaid debts, dishonest actions, and harm caused during active addiction.

declining.definition: Refusing or rejecting; turning down. AA should decline outside contributions.

decry.definition: To publicly criticize, condemn, or speak against something. 'We do not decry professionalism in other fields' — AA does not attack or look down on paid professional work in medicine, law, or religion. It simply recognizes that paying people to carry AA's message destroys its spiritual effectiveness.

dedication.definition: Wholehearted devotion; commitment. Dedication to spiritual principles.

defeat.definition: To win a victory over; to overcome. Complete defeat is the beginning of recovery in AA.

defects.definition: Flaws or weaknesses in character. Not fatal flaws, but areas where spiritual growth is needed.

defects of character.definition: Character flaws or weaknesses that cause problems - like selfishness, dishonesty, fear, and anger. Not moral evil, but human weaknesses that need spiritual change.

deferred.definition: Put off to a later time; postponed. Some amends must be deferred if timing is not right.

defiantly.definition: In open and bold resistance; refusing to submit to rules, warnings, or authority. 'We have defiantly played with every brand of fire' — describes how AA as a fellowship boldly tested every deviation from its principles, refusing to be told it couldn't try something, yet surviving intact.

deflate.definition: To reduce in size or volume; to reduce pride or confidence. Steps deflate the ego, which is necessary for recovery.

deformities.definition: Distortions or abnormalities in shape or character; malformations. In the 12x12, used metaphorically to describe the warped or twisted character defects that result from self-centered living.

degree.definition: A unit of measurement; an extent or level. Even a small degree of humility is necessary for sobriety.

dependability.definition: Quality of being dependable; reliability. Becoming dependable in recovery.

dependence.definition: Reliance on another person or substance; being dependent. Alcoholism creates unhealthy dependence.

deprecate.definition: To express disapproval of; to belittle or disparage. In the 12x12, used in the context of excessive self-deprecation, the practice of putting oneself down in an unhealthy way.

depress.definition: To make someone feel despondent or low in spirits; to push down. In the 12x12, used to describe how dwelling on one's shortcomings without spiritual perspective can lead to despair.

derelictions.definition: Neglect of one's duty or responsibilities; failures to fulfill obligations. In the 12x12, refers to the moral and practical failures that result from alcoholic self-centeredness.

derelicts.definition: People who have abandoned their responsibilities and are living in neglect; down-and-out individuals. In the 12x12, used to describe those at the extreme of social degradation due to alcoholism.

desire to stop drinking.definition: The sole requirement for AA membership. The only criterion for joining AA.

despairing.definition: Losing or having lost all hope; in a state of hopelessness. In the 12x12, describes the emotional depth reached by alcoholics before surrender and the beginning of recovery.

devastating.definition: Highly destructive or damaging; overwhelming. The devastating effects of alcoholism on self and others.

development.definition: Process of growing or improving. Spiritual development through Twelve Steps.

deviates.definition: Departs from an established course, standard, or expectation. In the 12x12, used to describe the departure from spiritual principles that leads back toward danger.

deviation.definition: A departure from an established course or standard. Every conceivable deviation from the Steps and Traditions has been tried.

devotion.definition: Love, loyalty, or enthusiasm for someone or something. Devotion to recovery.

dignity.definition: Worth and honor; quality of deserving respect. All have dignity regardless of past.

dilemma.definition: A situation requiring a difficult choice between equally undesirable options. The conflict newcomers feel about believing in a Higher Power.

direct amends.definition: Face-to-face restitution or apology to someone harmed. Distinguished from indirect or written amends.

direction.definition: Course or path; guidance. AA gets its direction from a loving God, not from human leaders.

directive.definition: An instruction or order. No AA member can give another a directive or enforce obedience.

directors.definition: People who supervise or manage. No board of directors with punishment power in AA.

disagreeable.definition: Unpleasant, offensive, or causing discomfort; difficult to get along with. In the 12x12, describes attitudes and behaviors that estrange others and damage relationships.

disappointingly.definition: In a manner that fails to meet expectations or wishes. Used in the 12x12 to describe the gap between what was hoped for and what was achieved in recovery efforts.

discernment.definition: The ability to see and understand clearly. Spiritual discernment means seeing deeper truths beyond just facts.

disciplinarians.definition: People who enforce strict rules and discipline. In the 12x12, referenced in the context of parenting styles or authority figures to illustrate the balance between structure and compassion.

discipline.definition: Training to ensure obedience; self-control. Self-discipline in recovery.

discloses.definition: Reveals or makes known previously hidden information. In the 12x12, refers to the courageous act of revealing one's wrongs and character defects in Step Five.

disclosure.definition: Making something known; revealing. Disclosure in Step Five.

discolored.definition: Changed to a different, less attractive color; stained. In the 12x12, used metaphorically to describe how character defects taint and mar the spiritual life.

disconcerting.definition: Causing discomfort, unease, or loss of composure. In the 12x12, describes experiences in self-examination or Step work that disturb complacency and invite growth.

discouragement.definition: The loss of confidence or enthusiasm; a feeling of despair. In the 12x12, identified as a spiritual danger that can stall recovery if not countered with hope and fellowship.

disenchanted.definition: Disappointed by discovering the truth about something previously idealized; disillusioned. In the 12x12, describes the experience of finding that worldly ambitions and pursuits cannot satisfy the soul.

disillusionment.definition: The feeling of disappointment resulting from the discovery that something is not as good as one believed. In the 12x12, describes a necessary spiritual experience that opens the door to genuine, humble seeking.

disliking.definition: Having an aversion to; feeling unfavorably toward. In the 12x12, used in the context of resentments and interpersonal tensions that need to be examined and released.

dismayed.definition: Feeling concern and distress; shocked and discouraged. In the 12x12, describes the reaction to seeing one's own character defects clearly for the first time.

dispelled.definition: Driven away or eliminated; made something vanish. In the 12x12, describes how honest examination, prayer, and fellowship can dispel fear, doubt, and spiritual darkness.

dissenters.definition: People who oppose or disagree with an official view or majority opinion. In the 12x12, referenced in the context of AA's group conscience processes where all voices deserve consideration.

distasteful.definition: Causing discomfort or strong dislike; unpleasant. In the 12x12, describes the unpleasant but necessary tasks, like making amends, that recovery requires.

distilling.definition: Purifying or concentrating through a heating and cooling process; extracting the essential quality. In the 12x12, used metaphorically to describe refining understanding or wisdom from experience.

distressingly.definition: In a manner that causes great suffering or anguish. Used in the 12x12 to emphasize the painfulness of certain character defects or life experiences.

distrust.definition: Lack of trust or confidence. Fear of public distrust and contempt.

divert.definition: To turn away from a course or purpose; to redirect attention or resources elsewhere. 'Lest problems of money, property and prestige divert us from our primary purpose' — meaning to pull AA away from its core mission of helping alcoholics recover. Tradition Six guards against this distraction.

divine guidance.definition: Direction provided by God or Higher Power. What AA seeks through prayer and meditation.

doctor.definition: A medical professional. Used metaphorically for those working in AA recovery.

doeth.definition: Archaic third-person singular present tense of 'do.' In the 12x12, appears in quoted scripture or elevated language to convey timeless spiritual truth.

dope addiction.definition: Addiction to narcotic or habit-forming drugs. Area where AA thought it might help but should not expand.

doubter.definition: A person who is uncertain or skeptical; one who questions. In the 12x12, addresses those who struggle to believe in a Higher Power, affirming that doubt is a legitimate starting point.

doubters.definition: People who are skeptical or uncertain, especially about spiritual matters. In the 12x12, addressed with compassion, as AA acknowledges that many arrive with deep skepticism.

dramatize.definition: To present something in a vivid, theatrical way that emphasizes emotional impact; to exaggerate. In the 12x12, used to describe the alcoholic's tendency to blow situations out of proportion for effect.

drearily.definition: In a dull, bleak, and uninteresting manner. Used in the 12x12 to describe the gray, joyless existence that can mark alcoholic life or early recovery without spiritual connection.

drifter.definition: A person who continually moves from place to place without a fixed home or purpose. In the 12x12, used to describe the spiritually rootless person who has not yet found stability in recovery.

drinkers.definition: Those who drink alcohol; alcoholics. Problem drinkers that AA members help.

drinking.definition: The act of consuming alcohol; also refers to the disease of alcoholism and compulsive alcohol use.

drunkards.definition: People who are habitually drunk; heavy drinkers. In the 12x12, used as a straightforward historical or biblical term for those whose lives are controlled by alcohol.

drunkenness.definition: The state of being intoxicated; habitual heavy drinking. In the 12x12, referenced as the condition from which AA members recover and that destroys individuals and families.

drunks.definition: People who are intoxicated or habitual drinkers. In AA, used as a plain, unsentimental self-description that members often use about themselves.

dry drunk.definition: A person sober from alcohol but not working the spiritual principles; still behaving irresponsibly and selfishly. Sobriety without spiritual change; just stopped drinking, not recovered.

drys.definition: People who abstain from alcohol; supporters of prohibition or temperance. In the 12x12, refers to the abstinent or prohibitionist camp in the historical debate about alcohol.

dubiously.definition: In a manner expressing doubt or skepticism; hesitantly. Used in the 12x12 to describe the tentative, uncertain approach of the newcomer to spiritual ideas.

duty.definition: A responsibility or commitment; what one must do. Duty to fellowship and recovery.

earnestly.definition: In a sincere and serious manner; with genuine commitment. In the 12x12, describes the quality of effort required in prayer, self-examination, and Twelfth Step work.

earnestness.definition: Sincerity and serious conviction; deep commitment to purpose. In the 12x12, identified as the quality of spiritual seeking and honest effort essential to recovery.

ebbed.definition: Gradually diminished or receded; fell back. In the 12x12, used to describe how enthusiasm, hope, or spiritual vitality can wane without ongoing effort and connection.

effort.definition: Exertion of physical or mental power. United effort is required to accomplish the cure.

ego deflation.definition: The reduction of excessive pride or self-regard. Necessary for spiritual growth and recovery.

egomania.definition: Obsessive preoccupation with oneself; extreme egotism. In the 12x12, used to describe the extreme self-centeredness at the core of alcoholic thinking.

egos.definition: The self, especially as distinct from the world; self-esteem. All Twelve Steps deflate the ego, which is necessary for recovery.

egotism.definition: An excessive sense of self-importance; the tendency to think and talk about oneself excessively. In the 12x12, identified as a central character defect that underlies much of the alcoholic's self-centered behavior.

elated.definition: In high spirits; very happy and excited. In the 12x12, describes the joy of spiritual breakthroughs, answered prayer, or the relief of honest self-disclosure.

eloquence.definition: Fluent, expressive, and persuasive speaking or writing. In the 12x12, referenced in the context of AA sharing and testimony that moves others toward recovery.

elude.definition: To escape or avoid by skill or cunning; to fail to be grasped or understood. In the 12x12, describes how serenity, happiness, or spiritual understanding can remain out of reach for the self-reliant.

embattled.definition: Involved in or surrounded by conflict, controversy, and fighting; beset by opposing forces. 'An embattled world' — the world as it is, full of political, religious, social, and ideological battles. AA chooses to remain neutral rather than taking sides in any of these conflicts.

emerge.definition: To come out from a difficult or dangerous situation; to appear on the other side of a trial. 'Only to emerge unharmed' — AA survived every experiment and test of its principles, coming through without lasting damage, stronger for the experience.

emergency.definition: A serious situation requiring immediate action. Faith can sustain one through any emergency.

emotional hangover.definition: Lingering emotional distress from past emotional excess. Distinguished from physical hangover.

emotional problem.definition: Psychological distress or mental health issues. Nearly every serious emotional problem stems from misdirected instinct.

emotional sobriety.definition: Being mentally and emotionally stable and clear - not just staying sober from alcohol. Finding peace and balance in how you think and feel.

emotionalism.definition: Excessive or uncontrolled emotional expression; allowing feelings to overwhelm reason. In the 12x12, identifies a danger when spiritual experience is sought through emotional intensity rather than genuine spiritual work.

empathy.definition: Understanding and sharing another person's feelings. Feeling with someone.

employ.definition: To hire someone to do paid work; to engage a person's services for wages. 'Our service centers may employ special workers' — Tradition Eight makes a distinction: AA's Twelfth Step work must remain unpaid, but service offices may hire paid administrative and support staff.

encouragement.definition: Support and hope given to someone; words or actions that inspire. Uplifting support.

endorse.definition: To approve and support; to sign the back of a check. To back or advocate.

enforce.definition: To compel obedience; to make someone follow a rule or command through authority or force. 'No one can give another a directive and enforce obedience' — AA has no authority to make any member comply with anything, which astonishes outsiders accustomed to hierarchical organizations.

enterprise.definition: A project, undertaking, or business venture, especially one with a commercial dimension. 'Any related facility or outside enterprise' — Tradition Six warns AA against attaching its name to outside businesses or projects, even ones that appear to serve a good purpose, because they can compromise AA's primary mission.

entirely.definition: Completely; fully; without any reservation or exception. The word 'entirely' is crucial in Step Six: the Step asks for complete and unconditional readiness — not a partial or selective willingness, but a total openness to having all character defects removed.

entitled.definition: Having earned the right to something; deserving of a particular recognition or benefit. 'Therefore entitled to be called' — such a person has genuinely earned the description of one who is sincerely trying to grow spiritually, not merely claiming it.

entreat.definition: To ask someone earnestly or anxiously; to beg or plead. In the 12x12, describes the sincere, humble appeal in prayer or in asking for help from others.

envies.definition: Feels resentful longing for another's possessions, qualities, or success. In the 12x12, identified as a character defect arising from the instinct for prestige and security gone awry.

envisions.definition: Imagines or pictures a future possibility in one's mind. In the 12x12, describes the practice of holding a spiritual vision of what recovery and character development can achieve.

equality.definition: State of being equal; treating all with same respect and dignity. All AA members are equal.

equilibrium.definition: State of balance; stable condition. Emotional equilibrium through recovery.

erring.definition: Having made a mistake; gone astray from the right path; in the wrong. An archaic and formal word for someone who has erred. 'Cast an erring member into outer darkness' — AA has no authority to expel or punish any member who goes wrong, unlike traditional organizations.

essays.definition: Short prose writings on a particular subject, presenting the author's reflections and views. The chapters of the 12x12 are described as essays — thoughtful, exploratory writings on each Step and Tradition, not rigid rules, but reflective pieces meant to provoke thought and discussion.

essence.definition: The most important element; basic nature. Essence of AA principles.

esteem.definition: High regard or respect; value and appreciation. Self-esteem returns through recovery.

evading.definition: Escaping or avoiding by cleverness or trickery; dodging. In the 12x12, describes the alcoholic's pattern of dodging responsibility and honest self-examination.

evaporates.definition: Disappears or becomes nothing; turns from liquid to vapor. In the 12x12, used to describe how fear, resentment, or spiritual obstacles disappear with honest action and prayer.

event.definition: An occurrence or happening. The event of public knowledge would harm AA.

evolution.definition: Gradual development or change. As person evolves through recovery.

exaggeration.definition: Representation of something as greater than reality. No exaggeration that publicity brings members.

excellence.definition: State of being outstanding or excellent. Striving for excellence in recovery work.

exclaim.definition: To cry out suddenly in surprise, shock, or strong emotion; to say something with force and feeling. 'Our friends gasp and exclaim, “This simply can't be”' — outsiders are so astonished by AA's non-hierarchical structure that they react with verbal surprise.

exert.definition: To apply effort or force; to make an effort. People must exert themselves to be secure and survive.

exertion.definition: Physical or mental effort; strenuous activity. In the 12x12, describes the work required in the Twelve Steps and in maintaining spiritual fitness.

exhibitionism.definition: Extravagant behavior designed to attract attention; a need for excessive admiration. In the 12x12, identified as a manifestation of egotism and the demand for prestige.

expediters.definition: People who make processes happen more quickly and efficiently; facilitators. In the 12x12, used to describe AA members who help smooth the path toward sobriety for others.

expel.definition: To drive out; to force something to leave entirely. 'Can expel the obsession to drink' — the Twelve Steps, if practiced as a way of life, can drive the obsession to drink completely out of a person's mind. This is stronger than merely managing or suppressing the obsession.

experience.definition: The accumulated knowledge and practical understanding from lived events. Experience is how AA members learn - 'Proved beyond doubt by immense experience' is the foundation of the program.

explicit.definition: Clear, detailed, and direct; leaving no room for ambiguity or misunderstanding. 'It presents an explicit view of the principles' — the 12x12 lays out the principles of AA plainly and in detail, rather than leaving them implied or vague.

expression.definition: Communication of ideas or feelings; manifestation. God expresses Himself through group conscience.

extemporaneously.definition: Done or spoken without preparation; spontaneously. In the 12x12, describes the kind of heartfelt, unrehearsed sharing that often proves most effective in AA meetings.

extolled.definition: Praised enthusiastically; spoke highly of. In the 12x12, used to describe how the AA program and its principles are commended and celebrated.

extramarital.definition: Occurring outside of marriage; relating to a romantic or sexual relationship outside one's marriage. In the 12x12, referenced as one area where the sex instinct can cause serious harm to others.

extravagance.definition: Lack of restraint in spending money or using resources; reckless excess. In the 12x12, used to describe the alcoholic's impulsive, self-indulgent approach to money and pleasure.

extricate.definition: To free someone from a constraint or difficulty; to disentangle. In the 12x12, describes the process of working free from the trap of character defects, resentments, or self-will.

extricated.definition: Freed from a constraint or difficult situation. In the 12x12, describes the liberation achieved through honest Step work and surrender to a Higher Power.

exult.definition: To show or feel triumphant joy or excitement. In the 12x12, describes the joy of genuine spiritual progress and the experience of freedom from the bondage of self.

face-to-face work.definition: Direct personal contact and interaction. Recovery work must be based on desire to help, not payment.

facts.definition: Things known to be true; reality. The facts of AA life are based on collective experience.

fairness.definition: Impartial treatment; lack of bias or favoritism. In dealing with all persons harmed.

faith.definition: Trust or confidence in a power greater than oneself; belief that recovery is possible through spiritual principles.

faithfulness.definition: Quality of being faithful; reliability. Faithful practice of Steps.

fallacies.definition: Mistaken beliefs or faulty reasoning; errors in logic. In the 12x12, refers to the distorted thinking patterns—such as self-sufficiency and rationalization—that sustain alcoholism.

fallible.definition: Capable of making mistakes or being wrong. In the 12x12, acknowledges human imperfection and the need for humility, recognizing that no person or plan is infallible.

falteringly.definition: In an unsteady or hesitant manner; with stops and stumbles. Used in the 12x12 to describe the imperfect, uncertain but sincere efforts of early recovery.

falters.definition: Loses strength or momentum; hesitates or stumbles. In the 12x12, describes the point where spiritual effort or commitment weakens and must be renewed.

families.definition: Groups related by blood or marriage. Inquiries come from alcoholics and their families.

fear.definition: An emotion of dread or apprehension. Fear prevents many from taking Step Five and from trying new things in recovery. Different from self-centered fear.

fearfully.definition: In a manner showing fear or anxiety; timidly. In the 12x12, describes the timid, anxious approach of those who have not yet been freed from fear through spiritual practice.

fearless.definition: Honest despite fear of judgment or shame. Not lacking fear, but willing to face facts anyway. A necessary willingness to look at uncomfortable truths about oneself.

fearlessly.definition: Without fear; courageously. In the 12x12, describes the quality of searching self-examination in Step Four, which must be done 'fearlessly and thoroughly.'

fellowship.definition: Friendly association with others; community of shared purpose. In AA, fellowship is both the feeling of belonging among people with the same problem and the practical support of attending meetings, having relationships, and being part of something larger than yourself.

Fellowship.name: Fellowship
Fellowship.definition: The AA community; the collective body of all AA members worldwide working together for recovery.

festering.definition: Becoming increasingly worse; continuing to cause harm or resentment internally. In the 12x12, describes how unaddressed resentments and wrongs continue to rot and poison the spirit.

festers.definition: Becomes increasingly worse or more bitter; suppurates with resentment or infection. In the 12x12, used to describe how buried resentments continue to cause internal harm if not addressed.

fevered.definition: Affected by or showing symptoms of fever; abnormally intense or excited. In the 12x12, describes the frantic, overheated quality of self-will-driven thought and craving.

films.definition: Moving pictures or cinema. Anonymity in public media like films.

finance.definition: To provide funds or capital for something. AA should never finance outside facilities or enterprises.

firecrackers.definition: Small explosive devices that make a loud bang when ignited. In the 12x12, used metaphorically to describe explosive outbursts of anger or impulsive behavior.

firmness.definition: Quality of being firm; strength. Firm foundation in principles.

firms.definition: Business organizations. Employers in firms want to know about alcoholism treatment.

fitful.definition: Occurring in irregular bursts; not steady or regular. In the 12x12, describes the inconsistent, stop-and-start quality of spiritual effort before a stable foundation is established.

flickering.definition: Shining unsteadily; wavering or fluctuating. In the 12x12, describes the faint, unstable quality of early faith or hope that needs to be tended and strengthened.

flier.definition: A person who flies; one who moves swiftly. In the 12x12, referenced colloquially, possibly meaning a handbill or as a metaphor for swift, reckless action.

flocked.definition: Gathered together in large numbers; congregated. In the 12x12, describes how people are drawn to AA and the message of recovery.

flourishing.definition: Thriving and developing well. How members flourish in recovery.

flowered.definition: Developed fully; blossomed. In the 12x12, used to describe the full, rich development of spiritual qualities and character in sustained recovery.

focus.definition: Center of activity or attention; concentration. Focusing on recovery work.

folk.definition: People or persons. Business and professional folk couldn't carry on occupations if known.

food.definition: Nourishment needed to sustain life. Harvesting food is a basic survival need.

foolishly.definition: In a foolish or unwise manner; recklessly; without good judgment. 'Isn't such liberty foolishly dangerous?' — the 12x12 poses this question that outsiders ask about AA's Tradition Three, which grants membership to any alcoholic regardless of behavior. The answer given is that AA's history shows this openness has not proven dangerous.

foolproof.definition: Incapable of going wrong; so simple or reliable that it cannot be misunderstood or misused. In the 12x12, used to describe the idealistic but mistaken belief that any human plan or system is infallible.

foregather.definition: To assemble or gather together, especially for a common purpose. In the 12x12, describes the coming together of AA members for fellowship, meetings, and shared purpose.

foresaw.definition: Saw or knew about something before it happened; predicted. In the 12x12, used to describe the capacity of wise AA members or the program's founders to anticipate needs and challenges.

foreword.definition: An introductory section at the beginning of a book, typically explaining the book’s purpose, background, or context — often written by someone other than the main author. “Its foreword made this revealing statement” — the Big Book’s own foreword candidly admitted the fear-based reasoning behind AA’s early approach to anonymity.

forgetfulness.definition: The tendency to forget things; failure to remember. In the 12x12, discussed in terms of willful forgetfulness of one's own faults while remembering others' wrongs.

forthright.definition: Direct and outspoken; candid and honest. In the 12x12, describes the quality of honest, direct communication required in recovery and Step work.

forthwith.definition: Immediately; without delay. In the 12x12, used to describe the urgency of making amends or taking necessary action when the time is right.

fortify.definition: To strengthen against attack or difficulty; to make more resistant. In the 12x12, describes how spiritual practice, prayer, and fellowship fortify the recovering person against temptation and difficulty.

foul.definition: Bad; unpleasant; stormy or adverse. Used in the traditional idiom 'in fair weather or foul' — meaning under all conditions, whether life is going well or badly. Step Ten asks AA members to apply their program every day, regardless of circumstances.

foundation.definition: A base or groundwork. Anonymity is the spiritual foundation of all traditions.

foundering.definition: Struggling and in danger of failing; filling with water and sinking. In the 12x12, describes the state of being in serious difficulty and about to collapse without intervention.

foursquare.definition: Firmly and resolutely; squarely and honestly, without equivocation. In the 12x12, describes the direct, unequivocal commitment to honesty and spiritual principles.

frankness.definition: The quality of being open, honest, and direct in expression. In the 12x12, identified as essential to honest self-examination and the kind of sharing that helps others in recovery.

freedom.definition: State of being free; liberty. Freedom from alcohol and its consequences.

frustrate.definition: To prevent from succeeding or to cause feelings of dissatisfaction. In the 12x12, describes how self-will and pride block spiritual growth and create persistent dissatisfaction.

fulfillment.definition: Completion or realization of something desired. Of purpose and potential.

fumbled.definition: Handled something clumsily or unsuccessfully; failed awkwardly. In the 12x12, describes the clumsy, imperfect early attempts at Step work or spiritual practice.

functions.definition: Purposes or intended uses. Instincts have proper functions that go wrong when misdirected.

furors.definition: Waves of enthusiastic admiration or public excitement; outbursts of fury. In the 12x12, describes the volatile emotional outbursts or surges of passion characteristic of alcoholic instability.

future.definition: Time that is to come. People have confidence in AA's future.

gain.definition: Something obtained or acquired; profit. Gains from recovery work.

garbled.definition: Confused or distorted in meaning; incomprehensibly mixed up. In the 12x12, describes muddled thinking or communication that results from distorted character or spiritual illness.

gasp.definition: To inhale sharply in shock or surprise; to catch one's breath suddenly. 'Our friends gasp and exclaim' — the reaction of astonishment from outsiders when they learn that AA has no authority to enforce anything. The gasp reflects genuine disbelief.

gaspers.definition: Slang for cigarettes; or people who gasp in shock. In the 12x12, used colloquially, likely referring to cigarettes as a common accompaniment to early AA meetings.

gentleness.definition: Soft, mild, or tender quality. Approach to others in recovery.

genuineness.definition: Being authentic or real; genuine quality. Must be genuine in recovery work.

ghosts.definition: Haunting memories or regrets from the past. The 'tormenting ghosts of yesterday' that haunt alcoholics.

gift.definition: A present or offering; something given freely. AA's unity is God's gift to the fellowship.

gloated.definition: Dwelt on one's own success or another's misfortune with smug satisfaction. In the 12x12, describes the superior, self-satisfied attitude that results from pride and lack of compassion.

gloried.definition: Took great pride or pleasure in something; basked in glory. In the 12x12, describes the self-congratulatory satisfaction that can become a spiritual pitfall.

gluttonous.definition: Excessively greedy or indulgent, especially with food or drink. In the 12x12, used to describe the lack of moderation and self-restraint that characterizes alcoholic excess.

gluttony.definition: Habitual greed or excess in eating or drinking. In the 12x12, identified as a manifestation of the deeper spiritual disorder of wanting more than one's fair share.

gnawing.definition: Persistently troubling or causing distress; biting slowly and steadily. In the 12x12, describes the persistent pain of unresolved guilt, resentment, or spiritual unease.

goaded.definition: Provoked or stimulated to action, especially through irritation. In the 12x12, describes how pride, fear, or resentment can push the alcoholic into self-destructive behavior.

god.definition: In AA, referred to as 'God as we understood Him.' Not necessarily religious; represents a power greater than oneself.

God as we understood Him.definition: A foundational phrase meaning each person can define God/Higher Power according to their own beliefs. The phrase allows atheists, agnostics, and religious members to all find their own understanding without requiring a specific deity belief.

God's will.definition: In AA, refers to the divine guidance or direction that comes from a power greater than oneself. Central to Step Eleven, which seeks knowledge of God's will for us and the power to carry it out. The direction or purpose that comes from surrendering to a power greater than oneself; accepting what the Higher Power intends for one's life rather than insisting on one's own desires.

got us over the barrel.definition: Trapped us in an impossible position with no good options (a common expression meaning someone is helpless or cornered). In the 12x12, AA acknowledges it's trapped newcomers: admitting powerlessness over alcohol, then saying only a Power greater than yourself can help.

grace.definition: Divine or spiritual favor; a gift from God. Life-giving grace of God flows through AA.

grandiose.definition: Extravagantly grand or ambitious; impressive in an exaggerated way. In the 12x12, describes the inflated, unrealistic thinking that leads alcoholics to overestimate themselves.

grandiosity.definition: An inflated, unrealistic sense of one's own importance, abilities, or achievements. In AA, identified as a key character defect that drives self-centered thinking and blocks humility.

grasp.definition: Understanding or comprehension; firm hold. Grasp of recovery.

gratitude.definition: Appreciation for benefits received; thankfulness. Essential for acknowledging God's grace.
